scenario_definitions.title: Identity Impersonation
scenario_definitions.summary: Active impersonation or identity fraud detected.
scenario_definitions.description: Someone is actively impersonating the victim or using their identity fraudulently. This triggers specific criminal provisions under the Online Safety Act.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Online impersonation is directly criminalised under OSA Section 18. Identity fraud using personal data also triggers data protection and computer crime provisions.

scenario_definitions.title: Image / Photo Abuse
scenario_definitions.summary: Personal images shared or misused without consent.
scenario_definitions.description: Personal photographs or images have been shared publicly without the victim's consent, potentially on an online platform. This constitutes a violation of privacy and may involve harassment.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: The unauthorized sharing or misuse of personal images is addressed by online safety provisions and data protection obligations regarding sensitive personal data.

scenario_definitions.title: Account Misuse / Takeover
scenario_definitions.summary: Account credentials or access channels compromised.
scenario_definitions.description: Account handles and contact information are exposed together, creating risk of unauthorized account access, credential stuffing, or account hijacking.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: This clause protects against unauthorized access to electronic systems, accounts, and data processing infrastructure.

scenario_definitions.title: Database / Mass Data Breach
scenario_definitions.summary: Large-scale data exposure with multiple PII categories.
scenario_definitions.description: Multiple categories of personal data have been exposed simultaneously, indicating a potential database breach or large-scale data leak affecting data integrity and confidentiality.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Data controllers have a legal obligation to protect personal data from breaches. Mass exposure of multiple PII categories constitutes a serious violation.

scenario_definitions.title: Unauthorized Data Processing
scenario_definitions.summary: Personal data processed or disclosed without lawful basis.
scenario_definitions.description: Personal data is being processed, stored, disclosed, or shared without a lawful basis or the data subject's consent. This includes doxxing, surveillance, and unlawful profiling.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Processing personal data without a lawful basis violates the data subject's fundamental rights under data protection legislation.

scenario_definitions.title: General Data Exposure
scenario_definitions.summary: Personal data has been exposed without authorization.
scenario_definitions.description: Personal data has been made accessible publicly or to unauthorized parties. The context suggests general exposure rather than a targeted attack.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Even general data exposure falls under data protection and computer crime provisions when personal data is shared without lawful basis.

scenario_definitions.title: Doxxing
scenario_definitions.summary: Personal information deliberately published to harass or endanger.
scenario_definitions.description: Private personal information has been deliberately published online to intimidate, harass, or endanger the victim. This may include addresses, phone numbers, workplace details, or family information.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Doxxing is addressed through online safety provisions criminalising harassment, data protection obligations, and computer crime provisions regarding unauthorized disclosure.

scenario_definitions.title: Online Harassment
scenario_definitions.summary: Sustained online harassment using personal data.
scenario_definitions.description: The victim is experiencing persistent online harassment, cyberbullying, or threatening behaviour that leverages their personal data. This may include repeated unwanted contact, threats, or intimidation.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Online harassment is criminalised under the Online Safety Act and may also engage telecommunications and data protection provisions.

scenario_definitions.title: Identity Theft
scenario_definitions.summary: Personal data stolen or misused for fraudulent purposes.
scenario_definitions.description: Sufficient personal data has been compromised to enable identity theft, including potential financial fraud, fraudulent account creation, or other unauthorized use of the victim's identity.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Identity theft engages both criminal provisions under the Computer Crime Act and data protection obligations under the PDPA.

scenario_definitions.title: Account Takeover
scenario_definitions.summary: Unauthorized access to accounts or digital systems.
scenario_definitions.description: An attacker has gained unauthorized access to the victim's online accounts, email, social media, or digital systems. This may involve credential theft, SIM swapping, or exploitation of security vulnerabilities.
scenario_definitions.clause_context: Account takeover is a criminal offence under the Computer Crime Act and Electronic Transactions Act, with additional protections under telecommunications legislation.
